pillar.metaDescription: Understand Medicare Part D prescription drug coverage in 2026: costs, the $2,100 cap, penalties and enrollment. Free help — call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).
pillar.h1: Medicare Part D: The Complete 2026 Prescription Drug Coverage Guide
pillar.quickAnswer: Medicare Part D is optional prescription drug coverage sold by private insurers approved by Medicare. In 2026, out-of-pocket drug costs are capped at $2,100 per year, the coverage gap (donut hole) is eliminated, and the base premium averages about $40/month, per CMS.
pillar.heroSubhead: Plans, costs, penalties and enrollment explained in plain English — with a licensed agent one call away at 1-800-MEDIGAP.
pillar.intro: Medicare Part D helps pay for the prescription drugs you fill at the pharmacy. This guide breaks down how it works in 2026 and how to choose a plan that fits your medications.

sections.h2: What is Medicare Part D and who needs it?
sections.body: Medicare Part D is voluntary prescription drug coverage offered through private insurance plans approved by Medicare. Original Medicare (Part A and Part B) does not cover most retail prescription drugs, so Part D fills that gap. You can get it as a standalone Prescription Drug Plan (PDP) that pairs with Original Medicare and a Medigap policy, or built into a Medicare Advantage plan (MA-PD). Roughly 50 million Americans have Part D coverage, according to KFF. Even if you take few medications today, enrolling when first eligible protects you from lifelong late penalties. A licensed agent at 1-800-MEDIGAP can confirm whether you need a plan.

sections.h2: How much does Medicare Part D cost in 2026?
sections.body: Part D costs include a monthly premium, an annual deductible, and copays or coinsurance per prescription. In 2026, the maximum deductible a plan can charge is $615, and the base beneficiary premium averages about $40 per month, per CMS. Premiums vary by plan and region. Higher-income enrollees pay an income-related monthly adjustment (IRMAA) on top of the premium. The biggest 2026 change: your total out-of-pocket spending on covered drugs is capped at $2,100, after which you pay $0 for covered medications for the rest of the year.

sections.h2: What changed for Part D in 2026?
sections.body: The Inflation Reduction Act reshaped Part D. As of 2025 the coverage gap, or donut hole, was eliminated, and for 2026 the annual out-of-pocket cap rises to $2,100. Insulin is capped at $35 per month, and many adult vaccines (like shingles) are free. The Medicare Prescription Payment Plan lets you spread your out-of-pocket drug costs into monthly installments instead of paying a large amount at the pharmacy. These protections apply to every Part D and Medicare Advantage drug plan nationwide.

sections.h2: When can you enroll in Medicare Part D?
sections.body: Your first chance is your Initial Enrollment Period — the seven-month window around your 65th birthday (three months before, your birthday month, and three months after). After that, the Annual Enrollment Period runs October 15 to December 7 each year, when you can join, switch, or drop a plan for the following year. If you miss your initial window and lack other creditable drug coverage, you may owe a permanent late enrollment penalty. Special Enrollment Periods exist for events like losing employer coverage or moving. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to find your window.

sections.h2: How do you choose the right Part D plan?
sections.body: The best plan is the one that covers your specific drugs at the lowest total yearly cost — not just the lowest premium. Start by listing your medications, then check each plan's formulary (its covered-drug list) and pricing tiers. A $0-premium plan can cost more overall if your drugs sit in expensive tiers or require prior authorization. Also confirm your pharmacy is in-network. Comparing plans every year matters because formularies and prices change annually. Our licensed agents at 1-800-MEDIGAP run your drug list against available plans at no cost to you.

faqs.q: Is Medicare Part D mandatory?
faqs.a: No. Part D is optional, but if you go without creditable drug coverage after becoming eligible, you may owe a permanent late enrollment penalty when you eventually sign up. Most people enroll when first eligible to avoid that lifelong cost, even if they take few medications.

faqs.q: What is the Part D out-of-pocket maximum in 2026?
faqs.a: In 2026, your out-of-pocket spending on covered Part D drugs is capped at $2,100. Once you reach it, you pay nothing for covered medications for the rest of the calendar year. This cap applies to all Part D and Medicare Advantage drug plans, per CMS.

faqs.q: Does Part D cover all my medications?
faqs.a: Not necessarily. Each plan has a formulary — its own list of covered drugs organized into cost tiers. Two plans can cover the same drug at very different prices, and some require prior authorization. Always check that your specific medications are covered before enrolling.

faqs.q: Can I have Part D with a Medigap plan?
faqs.a: Yes. If you have Original Medicare and a Medigap (Medicare Supplement) policy, you add a standalone Part D Prescription Drug Plan for your medications. Medigap policies sold today do not include drug coverage, so a separate Part D plan is how you cover prescriptions.

faqs.q: How much is the Part D late enrollment penalty?
faqs.a: The penalty equals 1% of the national base beneficiary premium for each full month you went without creditable drug coverage after eligibility. It is added to your premium permanently for as long as you have Part D, and it rises as the base premium changes each year.

faqs.q: What is the Medicare Prescription Payment Plan?
faqs.a: It is a 2025-and-later option that lets you pay your out-of-pocket Part D drug costs in monthly installments instead of all at once at the pharmacy. It does not lower your total cost, but it smooths large bills over the year. Enrollment is free and voluntary.

faqs.q: Did the donut hole go away?
faqs.a: Yes. The Part D coverage gap, known as the donut hole, was eliminated starting in 2025. For 2026, coverage moves through a deductible phase and an initial phase until you reach the $2,100 out-of-pocket cap, after which covered drugs cost you $0.

faqs.q: How do I get free help choosing a Part D plan?
faqs.a: Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427). A licensed agent will take your medication list, compare available plans in your area, and help you enroll at no cost. We also explain Extra Help, penalties, and how Part D works with Medigap or Medicare Advantage.

page.metaDescription: What is Medicare Part D? See how 2026 prescription drug coverage, costs and the $2,100 cap work. Free guidance — call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).
page.quickAnswer: Medicare Part D is optional prescription drug coverage from private insurers approved by Medicare. It pairs with Original Medicare or comes inside a Medicare Advantage plan. In 2026, out-of-pocket drug costs are capped at $2,100 and the donut hole is gone, per CMS.
page.heroSubhead: How Part D works in 2026 — and how to pick a plan that covers your drugs.
page.intro: Medicare Part D is how Original Medicare beneficiaries get prescription drug coverage. Here is what it covers, what it costs, and how to enroll.

sections.h2: What does Medicare Part D cover?
sections.body: Part D covers outpatient prescription drugs you fill at a pharmacy. Every plan must cover at least two drugs in each major therapeutic class and all drugs in six protected classes, such as cancer and HIV medications. Each plan publishes a formulary listing its covered drugs and cost tiers. Part D does not cover drugs administered in a doctor's office (those fall under Part B) or over-the-counter medicines. Because formularies differ, the same drug can cost very different amounts across plans. A licensed agent at 1-800-MEDIGAP can check your exact medications against available plans.

sections.h2: How do you get Part D coverage?
sections.body: There are two ways. First, a standalone Prescription Drug Plan (PDP) that attaches to Original Medicare — common for people who also carry a Medigap policy. Second, a Medicare Advantage plan that bundles drug coverage (MA-PD). You cannot have a standalone PDP and most Medicare Advantage plans at the same time. You enroll during your Initial Enrollment Period at 65, the Annual Enrollment Period (Oct 15–Dec 7), or a qualifying Special Enrollment Period. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP and we will confirm which path fits your situation.

sections.h2: What does Part D cost in 2026?
sections.body: Costs include a monthly premium, an annual deductible up to $615 in 2026, and copays per prescription. The national base premium averages about $40 per month, though plans vary. The major 2026 protection is a $2,100 annual out-of-pocket cap — after you spend that much on covered drugs, you pay $0 for the rest of the year. Insulin is capped at $35 per month and many vaccines are free. Higher earners pay an income-related surcharge (IRMAA) on top of their premium.

faqs.q: What is Medicare Part D in simple terms?
faqs.a: Medicare Part D is prescription drug insurance sold by private companies approved by Medicare. It helps pay for the medications you pick up at the pharmacy. You can buy it as a standalone plan with Original Medicare or get it built into a Medicare Advantage plan.

faqs.q: Is Part D included in Original Medicare?
faqs.a: No. Original Medicare (Parts A and B) does not include most prescription drug coverage. You add Part D separately through a private Prescription Drug Plan, or you get drug coverage through a Medicare Advantage plan that includes it.

faqs.q: How much does Medicare Part D cost per month?
faqs.a: Premiums vary by plan and region, with a 2026 national base of about $40 per month. You may also pay a deductible (up to $615 in 2026) and copays. Higher-income enrollees pay an additional income-related surcharge known as IRMAA.

faqs.q: Do I need Part D if I take no medications?
faqs.a: Many advisors recommend enrolling anyway. If you skip Part D without other creditable drug coverage, you face a permanent late enrollment penalty when you eventually join. A low-premium plan now can be cheaper than the penalty later. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to weigh your options.

faqs.q: Can I switch Part D plans?
faqs.a: Yes. You can change Part D plans each year during the Annual Enrollment Period, October 15 to December 7, with the new plan starting January 1. Some life events, like moving or losing other coverage, open a Special Enrollment Period to switch outside that window.

page.metaDescription: The Medicare Prescription Payment Plan spreads Part D drug costs into monthly bills. See how it works in 2026. Free help — call 1-800-MEDIGAP.
page.quickAnswer: The Medicare Prescription Payment Plan is a free, voluntary option that lets Part D enrollees spread out-of-pocket drug costs into monthly installments instead of paying large amounts at the pharmacy. It does not reduce your total cost, but it smooths big bills across the year, per Medicare.gov.
page.heroSubhead: Spread your Part D drug costs over the year instead of paying all at once.
page.intro: The Medicare Prescription Payment Plan helps people facing high pharmacy bills budget their drug costs across the calendar year. Here is how it works.

sections.h2: How does the Medicare Prescription Payment Plan work?
sections.body: Instead of paying your share at the pharmacy counter, your Part D plan pays the pharmacy and then bills you in monthly installments. Your total out-of-pocket cost for the year stays the same — capped at $2,100 in 2026 — but it is split into predictable monthly payments rather than large lump sums. The program is available through every Part D and Medicare Advantage drug plan, costs nothing to join, and charges no interest or fees. It is most useful if you have an expensive drug early in the year.

sections.h2: Who should consider this payment option?
sections.body: This option helps most if you face high drug costs, especially early in the calendar year before reaching the $2,100 cap. By spreading payments, you avoid a single large bill in January or February. It may not help those with low, steady drug costs spread evenly across the year. There is no income requirement and joining does not change which drugs are covered or their total price. To decide whether it fits your medications and budget, call 1-800-MEDIGAP for a free review.

faqs.q: Does the Medicare Prescription Payment Plan save me money?
faqs.a: No. It does not lower your total drug costs — you still pay the same amount over the year, up to the $2,100 cap in 2026. It only changes the timing, spreading what you owe into monthly installments instead of large payments at the pharmacy.

faqs.q: How do I sign up for the payment plan?
faqs.a: Contact your Part D or Medicare Advantage drug plan to opt in; every plan must offer it. You can join before the plan year or during it. Enrollment is free, requires no income check, and you can opt out at any time. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for step-by-step help.

faqs.q: Is there a fee or interest to use it?
faqs.a: No. The Medicare Prescription Payment Plan charges no interest, fees, or finance costs. You pay only what you would have owed at the pharmacy, just billed monthly by your plan instead of paid all at once.

faqs.q: Is the payment plan the same as Extra Help?
faqs.a: No. Extra Help is a low-income subsidy that actually lowers your premiums, deductibles, and copays. The Medicare Prescription Payment Plan only spreads your existing costs over the year — it does not reduce them. You can qualify for both if eligible.

page.metaDescription: Compare 2026 Medicare prescription drug plans: premiums, the $2,100 cap, formularies and enrollment dates. Free help — call 1-800-MEDIGAP.
page.quickAnswer: 2026 Medicare prescription drug plans are private Part D plans with a maximum $615 deductible, a $2,100 annual out-of-pocket cap, $35 insulin, and free recommended vaccines. Premiums vary by plan and region, averaging about $40/month for the base benefit, per CMS.
page.heroSubhead: What is new for 2026 plans and how to choose the right one for your drugs.
page.intro: Medicare prescription drug plans change every year. Here is what is new for 2026 and how to compare your options before enrollment closes.

sections.h2: What is new for 2026 prescription drug plans?
sections.body: For 2026, the annual out-of-pocket cap on covered drugs rises to $2,100, up from $2,000 in 2025. The maximum deductible a plan can charge is $615. The donut hole remains eliminated, insulin stays capped at $35 per month, and recommended adult vaccines are free. The Medicare Prescription Payment Plan continues, letting you pay out-of-pocket costs monthly. Plan premiums, formularies, and pharmacy networks reset each year, so the plan that fit you in 2025 may not be best for 2026.

sections.h2: How do I compare 2026 drug plans?
sections.body: Compare on total annual cost, not just premium. List your medications and check each plan's formulary, tier placement, deductible, and whether your pharmacy is in-network. A $0-premium plan can cost more if your drugs are on high tiers or need prior authorization. The Annual Enrollment Period runs October 15 to December 7, with coverage starting January 1, 2026. Because every plan files new pricing, reviewing your options each fall can save real money. Our licensed agents at 1-800-MEDIGAP run this comparison for free.

faqs.q: When can I enroll in a 2026 prescription drug plan?
faqs.a: The Annual Enrollment Period runs October 15 to December 7, 2025, for coverage that begins January 1, 2026. New Medicare beneficiaries can also enroll during their Initial Enrollment Period around their 65th birthday, or during a qualifying Special Enrollment Period.

faqs.q: What is the 2026 out-of-pocket maximum?
faqs.a: In 2026, your out-of-pocket spending on covered Part D drugs is capped at $2,100. After reaching it, you pay $0 for covered medications for the rest of the year. This cap applies to all Medicare prescription drug plans nationwide, per CMS.

faqs.q: How much will a 2026 drug plan cost?
faqs.a: Premiums vary by plan and region, averaging about $40 per month for the base benefit in 2026. Plans may also charge a deductible up to $615 plus copays. Higher-income enrollees pay an income-related surcharge (IRMAA) on top of the premium.

faqs.q: Should I switch plans for 2026?
faqs.a: Possibly. Plans change premiums, formularies, and pharmacy networks every year, so your current plan may no longer be the lowest-cost option for your drugs. Comparing plans each fall is the best way to confirm. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for a free side-by-side review.

faqs.q: Are vaccines and insulin covered in 2026?
faqs.a: Yes. In 2026, insulin remains capped at $35 per month with no deductible, and recommended adult vaccines, such as shingles, are covered at no cost under all Part D plans. These protections continue from the Inflation Reduction Act.

page.metaDescription: A Part D formulary is a plan's covered-drug list with cost tiers. Learn how it affects your prices in 2026. Free check — call 1-800-MEDIGAP.
page.quickAnswer: A Medicare Part D formulary is the list of prescription drugs a plan covers, organized into cost tiers. Formularies vary by plan, so the same drug can cost very different amounts. Plans must cover at least two drugs per class and all drugs in six protected classes, per CMS.
page.heroSubhead: How a plan's covered-drug list shapes what you pay at the pharmacy.
page.intro: Your Part D formulary determines whether your medications are covered and how much they cost. Here is how to read one before you enroll.

sections.h2: What is a Part D formulary?
sections.body: A formulary is the official list of drugs a Part D plan covers, divided into pricing tiers — typically preferred generics, generics, preferred brands, non-preferred drugs, and specialty drugs. Lower tiers cost less. Every plan must cover at least two drugs in each therapeutic category and all drugs in six protected classes, including antidepressants, antipsychotics, anticonvulsants, immunosuppressants, cancer drugs, and HIV/AIDS drugs. Because formularies differ widely, a drug that is cheap on one plan may be expensive or excluded on another. Always verify your medications before choosing a plan; 1-800-MEDIGAP can check them for you.

sections.h2: What if my drug is not on the formulary?
sections.body: If a plan does not cover your medication, you have options. You can ask your doctor about a covered alternative, request a formulary exception with supporting documentation, or choose a different plan that covers your drug. Some drugs are covered but carry restrictions like prior authorization, step therapy, or quantity limits. Formularies can change during the year, though plans must give notice. The simplest fix is comparing plans by your actual drug list each fall. A licensed agent at 1-800-MEDIGAP can find a plan that covers your prescriptions.

faqs.q: What does it mean if a drug is not on the formulary?
faqs.a: It means the plan does not normally cover that medication, so you would pay full price. You can request a formulary exception, switch to a covered alternative with your doctor, or choose a different plan that covers your drug. Restrictions like prior authorization may also apply.

faqs.q: Can a Part D formulary change during the year?
faqs.a: Yes. Plans can change their formularies during the year, such as adding generics or moving drugs between tiers, but they must generally notify affected members in advance. Most major changes take effect at the start of the next plan year on January 1.

faqs.q: What are the six protected drug classes?
faqs.a: Part D plans must cover essentially all drugs in six protected classes: antidepressants, antipsychotics, anticonvulsants, immunosuppressants (for transplants), cancer drugs, and HIV/AIDS drugs. This rule ensures patients with serious conditions retain access regardless of plan.

faqs.q: How do I find a plan that covers my drug?
faqs.a: List every medication you take, then compare plan formularies and tier pricing for those drugs. The lowest total annual cost — premium plus drug costs — wins, not the lowest premium alone. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP and a licensed agent will run your list against available plans for free.

page.metaDescription: The Part D late enrollment penalty is permanent and grows yearly. Learn how it is calculated and how to avoid it. Free help — call 1-800-MEDIGAP.
page.quickAnswer: The Part D late enrollment penalty is a permanent charge added to your premium for going without creditable drug coverage after eligibility. It equals 1% of the national base premium for each uncovered month, recalculated yearly as the base premium changes, per Medicare.gov.
page.heroSubhead: How the penalty is calculated, why it lasts for life, and how to avoid it.
page.intro: The Part D late enrollment penalty can quietly raise your premium for the rest of your life. Here is how it works and how to steer clear of it.

sections.h2: How is the Part D late enrollment penalty calculated?
sections.body: The penalty is 1% of the national base beneficiary premium multiplied by the number of full months you went without creditable drug coverage after your Initial Enrollment Period ended. Medicare rounds the result to the nearest 10 cents and adds it to your monthly Part D premium. Because it is tied to the national base premium, which changes each year, your penalty amount can rise over time. The penalty is permanent — it applies for as long as you have Part D coverage. A licensed agent at 1-800-MEDIGAP can estimate yours.

sections.h2: How do you avoid the penalty?
sections.body: Enroll in a Part D plan during your Initial Enrollment Period — the seven months around your 65th birthday — or keep other creditable drug coverage, such as from an employer, union, or VA benefits. Coverage is creditable if it is expected to pay, on average, at least as much as standard Part D. Keep the annual notice your plan sends confirming creditable status. If you lose creditable coverage, enroll within 63 days to avoid a penalty. Unsure if your coverage counts? Call 1-800-MEDIGAP before you decide.

faqs.q: How much is the Part D late enrollment penalty?
faqs.a: It equals 1% of the national base beneficiary premium for each full month you went without creditable drug coverage, rounded to the nearest 10 cents and added to your premium. Because the base premium changes yearly, the penalty amount can increase over time.

faqs.q: Is the Part D penalty permanent?
faqs.a: Yes, in most cases. The penalty is added to your Part D premium for as long as you have drug coverage. The main exceptions are qualifying for Extra Help, which eliminates the penalty, or being under 65 in certain situations. Otherwise it lasts for life.

faqs.q: How long can I go without Part D before being penalized?
faqs.a: If you have creditable drug coverage, you can delay Part D without penalty. Without creditable coverage, the penalty starts accruing once you go 63 or more days past your Initial Enrollment Period. Keeping creditable coverage is the key to avoiding it.

faqs.q: Can the Part D penalty be removed?
faqs.a: Sometimes. Qualifying for the Extra Help low-income subsidy eliminates the penalty. You can also request a reconsideration if you believe you had creditable coverage that was not counted. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP and we can help you review your situation.

faqs.q: Does Extra Help remove the late enrollment penalty?
faqs.a: Yes. If you qualify for the Extra Help low-income subsidy, Medicare waives the Part D late enrollment penalty for as long as you keep that assistance. This is one of several reasons to check your Extra Help eligibility.

children.keyword: medicare part d out of pocket maximum 2026

page.metaDescription: In 2026 the Medicare Part D out-of-pocket maximum is $2,100, then covered drugs cost $0. See how it works. Free help — call 1-800-MEDIGAP.
page.quickAnswer: In 2026, the Medicare Part D out-of-pocket maximum is $2,100. Once your spending on covered drugs reaches that cap, you pay $0 for covered medications for the rest of the year. The cap applies to every Part D and Medicare Advantage drug plan, per CMS.
page.heroSubhead: The 2026 cap that protects you from runaway prescription costs.
page.intro: The 2026 Part D out-of-pocket maximum is one of the most important protections in Medicare drug coverage. Here is exactly how the $2,100 cap works.

sections.h2: What counts toward the $2,100 cap?
sections.body: Your deductible, copays, and coinsurance on covered Part D drugs all count toward the $2,100 out-of-pocket maximum in 2026. Once your true out-of-pocket spending reaches that amount, you enter the catastrophic phase and pay nothing for covered medications for the rest of the calendar year. Monthly premiums do not count toward the cap. The cap resets each January 1. This protection, created by the Inflation Reduction Act, replaced the old donut hole and limits what any beneficiary pays for covered drugs in a year.

sections.h2: How does the cap help people with high drug costs?
sections.body: Before 2025, there was no annual ceiling, and people on expensive specialty drugs could pay many thousands of dollars. With the 2026 cap at $2,100, your worst-case spending on covered drugs is predictable and limited. If you face high costs early in the year, the Medicare Prescription Payment Plan lets you spread that $2,100 into monthly installments instead of paying it at once. The cap applies automatically — you do not need to enroll separately. Questions about how it affects your medications? Call 1-800-MEDIGAP.

faqs.q: What is the Part D out-of-pocket maximum for 2026?
faqs.a: The 2026 Part D out-of-pocket maximum is $2,100. After your spending on covered drugs reaches that amount, you pay $0 for covered medications for the rest of the year. The cap applies to all Part D and Medicare Advantage drug plans.

faqs.q: Does my premium count toward the $2,100 cap?
faqs.a: No. Only your deductible, copays, and coinsurance on covered drugs count toward the $2,100 out-of-pocket maximum. Monthly plan premiums do not count. Once your eligible out-of-pocket spending hits the cap, covered drugs cost you nothing for the rest of the year.

faqs.q: Does the cap reset each year?
faqs.a: Yes. The out-of-pocket maximum resets every January 1. Spending toward the cap does not carry over, so each calendar year you start again at zero and work toward the new annual limit, which is $2,100 in 2026.

faqs.q: Can I spread the $2,100 over the year?
faqs.a: Yes. The Medicare Prescription Payment Plan lets you pay your out-of-pocket drug costs in monthly installments rather than all at once at the pharmacy. It does not lower the $2,100 total but smooths large bills. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to learn how to enroll.

page.metaDescription: The Medicare donut hole is eliminated in 2026, replaced by a $2,100 out-of-pocket cap. See what changed. Free help — call 1-800-MEDIGAP.
page.quickAnswer: There is no donut hole in 2026. The Part D coverage gap was eliminated starting in 2025, replaced by a $2,100 annual out-of-pocket cap. After reaching it, you pay $0 for covered drugs for the rest of the year, per the Inflation Reduction Act.
page.heroSubhead: Why the coverage gap no longer exists and what replaced it.
page.intro: The Medicare donut hole confused beneficiaries for years. In 2026 it is gone for good. Here is what replaced it and what it means for your costs.

sections.h2: Is there still a donut hole in 2026?
sections.body: No. The donut hole — formally the Part D coverage gap — was permanently eliminated beginning in 2025. Previously, after you and your plan spent a set amount, you fell into a gap where you paid a larger share until catastrophic coverage kicked in. The Inflation Reduction Act removed that phase. For 2026, Part D has a simpler structure: a deductible phase, an initial coverage phase, and then catastrophic coverage once you hit the $2,100 out-of-pocket cap. After the cap, covered drugs cost you $0.

sections.h2: What replaced the donut hole?
sections.body: The coverage gap was replaced by a hard annual out-of-pocket maximum — $2,100 in 2026. Instead of dropping into a confusing gap with higher costs, your spending simply moves toward the cap, after which you pay nothing for covered drugs. Insulin stays capped at $35 monthly and recommended vaccines are free. If high early-year costs are a concern, the Medicare Prescription Payment Plan can spread your out-of-pocket spending across monthly installments. For help understanding your 2026 costs, call 1-800-MEDIGAP.

faqs.q: Does the Medicare donut hole still exist in 2026?
faqs.a: No. The Part D coverage gap, known as the donut hole, was eliminated starting in 2025 and does not exist in 2026. It was replaced by a $2,100 annual out-of-pocket cap, after which covered drugs cost you nothing for the rest of the year.

faqs.q: What replaced the donut hole?
faqs.a: A $2,100 out-of-pocket maximum replaced the coverage gap in 2026. Part D now has three phases: deductible, initial coverage, and catastrophic. Once your out-of-pocket spending reaches the cap, you enter catastrophic coverage and pay $0 for covered drugs.

faqs.q: Why was the donut hole eliminated?
faqs.a: The Inflation Reduction Act restructured Part D to protect beneficiaries from unpredictable, high drug costs. Removing the coverage gap and adding a firm out-of-pocket cap made spending simpler and more affordable, especially for people on expensive specialty medications.

faqs.q: Will my drug costs be lower without the donut hole?
faqs.a: For many people, yes — especially those with high drug spending who previously hit the gap. The $2,100 cap limits your worst-case annual cost on covered drugs. Your exact savings depend on your medications. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for a free cost review.

page.metaDescription: Creditable drug coverage pays as much as Part D and lets you delay enrolling without penalty. Learn how to check yours. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP.
page.quickAnswer: Creditable drug coverage is prescription coverage expected to pay, on average, at least as much as standard Medicare Part D. Having it lets you delay Part D without a late penalty. Employer, union, VA, and TRICARE plans are often creditable, per Medicare.gov.
page.heroSubhead: How to know if your current drug coverage lets you delay Part D penalty-free.
page.intro: Creditable drug coverage is the key to delaying Medicare Part D without a penalty. Here is what counts and how to confirm yours.

sections.h2: What makes drug coverage creditable?
sections.body: Coverage is creditable if it is expected to pay, on average, at least as much as standard Medicare Part D coverage. Common creditable sources include current employer or union group plans, retiree coverage, VA benefits, TRICARE, and the Federal Employees Health Benefits Program. Each year, your plan must send a Notice of Creditable Coverage stating whether it qualifies. Keep these notices — you may need them to prove you avoided a gap if you later enroll in Part D. If you are unsure whether your plan is creditable, a licensed agent at 1-800-MEDIGAP can help you check.

sections.h2: Why creditable coverage matters for penalties
sections.body: If you keep creditable drug coverage, you can delay enrolling in Part D without triggering the late enrollment penalty. But if you go 63 or more days without creditable coverage after becoming eligible, the penalty starts accruing and becomes permanent once you join Part D. When you lose creditable coverage — say, you retire — you generally get a Special Enrollment Period to join Part D within 63 days, penalty-free. Acting within that window is critical. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP as soon as your coverage changes so you do not miss it.

faqs.q: What is creditable drug coverage?
faqs.a: Creditable drug coverage is prescription coverage expected to pay, on average, at least as much as standard Medicare Part D. Having it lets you delay Part D enrollment without incurring the late enrollment penalty. Your plan sends an annual notice confirming whether it qualifies.

faqs.q: How do I know if my coverage is creditable?
faqs.a: Your plan must send a Notice of Creditable Coverage each year, usually before October 15, stating whether it qualifies. Check that notice, or ask your benefits administrator. Employer, union, VA, and TRICARE plans are often creditable. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP if you are unsure.

faqs.q: What happens if I lose creditable coverage?
faqs.a: You generally get a Special Enrollment Period to join a Part D plan within 63 days without penalty. Acting quickly is essential — waiting longer than 63 days can trigger the permanent late enrollment penalty. Keep your loss-of-coverage documentation as proof.

faqs.q: Is VA drug coverage creditable for Part D?
faqs.a: Yes. VA prescription drug benefits are considered creditable coverage, so veterans using VA pharmacy benefits can typically delay Part D without penalty. Some veterans still add Part D for access to retail pharmacies. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to weigh whether it makes sense for you.

children.keyword: medicare extra help low income subsidy

page.metaDescription: Medicare Extra Help (LIS) cuts Part D premiums, deductibles and copays for those with limited income. See benefits. Free help — call 1-800-MEDIGAP.
page.quickAnswer: Medicare Extra Help, also called the Low-Income Subsidy (LIS), lowers Part D premiums, deductibles, and copays for people with limited income and resources. In 2026 it can be worth roughly $5,900 a year and eliminates the late enrollment penalty, per the Social Security Administration.
page.heroSubhead: Major help with prescription costs for seniors with limited income.
page.intro: Medicare Extra Help can dramatically lower your prescription drug costs if you have limited income and savings. Here is what it covers and who qualifies.

sections.h2: What does Extra Help cover?
sections.body: Extra Help, the Part D Low-Income Subsidy, reduces or eliminates your Part D premium and deductible and caps your copays at low fixed amounts. The Social Security Administration estimates the benefit is worth about $5,900 per year. It also removes the Part D late enrollment penalty for as long as you keep it, and gives you a Special Enrollment Period to change plans. As of 2024, the program was expanded so more people qualify for the full subsidy. If you have limited income and resources, applying is well worth it — call 1-800-MEDIGAP for help.

sections.h2: How do you apply for Extra Help?
sections.body: You apply through the Social Security Administration online, by phone, or in person — there is no cost to apply. Some people qualify automatically: those with Medicaid, Supplemental Security Income (SSI), or a Medicare Savings Program are deemed eligible and do not need to apply separately. Everyone else submits an application reporting income and resources. If approved, the subsidy applies to whichever Part D plan you choose. A licensed agent at 1-800-MEDIGAP can walk you through the application and help you pick a plan that pairs well with Extra Help.

faqs.q: What is Medicare Extra Help?
faqs.a: Medicare Extra Help, or the Low-Income Subsidy, is a federal program that lowers Part D prescription drug costs — premiums, deductibles, and copays — for people with limited income and resources. The Social Security Administration estimates it is worth about $5,900 a year.

faqs.q: How much does Extra Help save me?
faqs.a: The Social Security Administration estimates Extra Help is worth roughly $5,900 per year. It can eliminate your Part D premium and deductible, cap your copays at low amounts, and remove the late enrollment penalty for as long as you qualify.

faqs.q: Does Extra Help remove the Part D penalty?
faqs.a: Yes. If you qualify for Extra Help, the Part D late enrollment penalty is waived for as long as you keep the subsidy. This is one of several valuable protections the program provides alongside lower premiums and copays.

faqs.q: Do I qualify automatically for Extra Help?
faqs.a: Some people do. If you have Medicaid, Supplemental Security Income (SSI), or a Medicare Savings Program, you are deemed automatically eligible and need not apply separately. Everyone else applies through Social Security. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to find out where you stand.

faqs.q: Where do I apply for Extra Help?
faqs.a: Apply through the Social Security Administration online, by phone, or at a local office — there is no fee. You report your income and resources, and if approved, the subsidy applies to your chosen Part D plan. A licensed agent at 1-800-MEDIGAP can guide you through it.

children.keyword: how to qualify for extra help

page.metaTitle: How to Qualify for Extra Help 2026 | 1-800-MEDIGAP
page.metaDescription: See the 2026 income and resource limits to qualify for Medicare Extra Help and how to apply. Free guidance — call 1-800-MEDIGAP.
page.h1: How to Qualify for Extra Help
page.quickAnswer: To qualify for Medicare Extra Help in 2026, your income must generally be below 150% of the federal poverty level with limited resources. People with Medicaid, SSI, or a Medicare Savings Program qualify automatically. Apply free through Social Security, per SSA guidelines.
page.heroSubhead: The income and resource rules — and how to apply for the subsidy.
page.intro: Qualifying for Extra Help comes down to income and resource limits. Here is who is eligible and how to apply for this valuable subsidy.

sections.h2: What are the income and resource limits?
sections.body: To qualify for the full Extra Help subsidy, your income generally must be below 150% of the federal poverty level, with countable resources under set limits that the government adjusts annually. Resources include savings, investments, and real estate other than your home; they do not count your house, car, or personal belongings. As of 2024, the program was expanded so the full subsidy reaches more people. Because limits change each year and counting rules can be tricky, it is worth applying even if you are unsure. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP and we will help you check.

sections.h2: Who qualifies automatically?
sections.body: You automatically qualify for Extra Help — no application needed — if you have full Medicaid, get Supplemental Security Income (SSI), or are enrolled in a Medicare Savings Program. These are called deemed-eligible groups, and Social Security mails you a notice confirming it. Everyone else must apply and report income and resources. Even if you were denied before, it is worth reapplying after the eligibility expansion. To confirm whether you already qualify automatically or should apply, a licensed agent at 1-800-MEDIGAP can review your situation at no cost.

faqs.q: What are the income limits for Extra Help?
faqs.a: To qualify for the full Extra Help subsidy in 2026, your income generally must be below 150% of the federal poverty level, with limited countable resources. Exact dollar limits adjust each year. Even if you are close, it is worth applying — call 1-800-MEDIGAP to check.

faqs.q: What counts as a resource for Extra Help?
faqs.a: Countable resources include money in checking and savings accounts, stocks, bonds, and real estate other than your primary home. Your house, one car, personal belongings, and life insurance generally do not count. Resource limits are set annually by the government.

faqs.q: Who automatically qualifies for Extra Help?
faqs.a: People with full Medicaid, Supplemental Security Income (SSI), or a Medicare Savings Program automatically qualify for Extra Help without applying. Social Security sends a notice confirming it. Everyone else must apply and report income and resources to be considered.

faqs.q: Should I reapply if I was denied before?
faqs.a: Yes. The Extra Help program was expanded so the full subsidy now reaches more people. If you were denied in the past, your circumstances or the limits may have changed. Reapplying is free. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for help submitting a new application.

faqs.q: How do I apply for Extra Help?
faqs.a: Apply through the Social Security Administration online, by phone, or in person at no cost. You report your income and resources, and Social Security determines eligibility. A licensed agent at 1-800-MEDIGAP can guide you through the application step by step.

page.metaTitle: Best Part D Plan 2026: How to Choose | 1-800-MEDIGAP
page.metaDescription: The best 2026 Part D plan is the one that covers your drugs at the lowest total cost. See how to find yours. Free help — call 1-800-MEDIGAP.
page.quickAnswer: The best 2026 Part D plan is the one that covers your specific medications at the lowest total annual cost — premium plus deductible plus copays — with your pharmacy in-network. There is no single best plan for everyone; it depends entirely on your drug list, per Medicare.gov.
page.heroSubhead: Why there is no one best plan — and how to find the best one for you.
page.intro: There is no universally best Part D plan for 2026. The right plan depends on your drugs. Here is how to find the one that costs you the least.

sections.h2: How do you find the best Part D plan for you?
sections.body: Start with your medication list, then compare plans on total annual cost — premium, deductible, and copays combined — not just the monthly premium. A $0-premium plan can be the most expensive choice if your drugs sit on high tiers or face restrictions. Confirm your pharmacy is in-network and that each drug is on the formulary without prior authorization hurdles. Check the plan's star rating for service quality. Because the cheapest plan is personal to your prescriptions, the smartest move is a free side-by-side comparison. Our licensed agents at 1-800-MEDIGAP do exactly that.

sections.h2: What should you watch out for?
sections.body: Avoid choosing by premium alone or by brand familiarity. Watch for high specialty-drug coinsurance, prior authorization and step therapy requirements, restrictive pharmacy networks, and formulary exclusions of your medications. Also confirm whether mail-order pricing beats retail for your drugs. Plans change every year, so even a great 2025 plan may not be your best 2026 option. The Annual Enrollment Period (Oct 15–Dec 7) is the time to compare. To avoid costly surprises, call 1-800-MEDIGAP and let a licensed agent match a plan to your exact medications.

faqs.q: What is the best Part D plan for 2026?
faqs.a: There is no single best plan for everyone. The best 2026 Part D plan is the one that covers your specific drugs at the lowest total annual cost with your pharmacy in-network. Because it depends on your medications, a personalized comparison is the only reliable way to find it.

faqs.q: Should I pick the plan with the lowest premium?
faqs.a: Not necessarily. A low or $0 premium plan can cost more overall if your drugs are on expensive tiers or carry restrictions. Compare total annual cost — premium plus deductible plus copays — for your actual drug list. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for a free comparison.

faqs.q: How do star ratings affect my choice?
faqs.a: Medicare star ratings measure plan quality and customer service on a 1-to-5 scale. Higher-rated plans tend to offer better service and fewer problems. Ratings are one factor, but covering your drugs at the lowest cost should still drive your decision.

faqs.q: How often should I review my Part D plan?
faqs.a: Every year. Plans change premiums, formularies, and pharmacy networks annually, so your best plan can shift from one year to the next. Review during the Annual Enrollment Period, October 15 to December 7. A licensed agent at 1-800-MEDIGAP can re-check it for free.

page.metaDescription: Compare 2026 Part D plans by your drug list, total cost and pharmacy network. Free side-by-side help — call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).
page.quickAnswer: To compare 2026 Part D plans, list your medications and rank plans by total annual cost — premium, deductible, and copays — confirming each drug is on the formulary and your pharmacy is in-network. The lowest total cost, not the lowest premium, wins, per Medicare.gov.
page.heroSubhead: A simple, drug-by-drug method to compare plans before enrollment closes.
page.intro: Comparing Part D plans the right way can save you hundreds of dollars in 2026. Here is a step-by-step method and the dates you need to know.

sections.h2: How do you compare 2026 Part D plans step by step?
sections.body: First, write down every medication, dose, and frequency. Second, gather candidate plans in your area. Third, for each plan check that all your drugs are on the formulary and note their tiers, then add up premium, deductible, and estimated copays for a true annual cost. Fourth, confirm your preferred pharmacy is in-network and compare mail-order pricing. Fifth, check the star rating. The plan with the lowest total cost that covers your drugs wins. This takes time — our licensed agents at 1-800-MEDIGAP run the full comparison for you at no charge.

sections.h2: When is the deadline to compare and switch?
sections.body: The Annual Enrollment Period runs October 15 to December 7, 2025, with your new plan starting January 1, 2026. This is the main window to compare and switch for the coming year. New beneficiaries also have their Initial Enrollment Period at 65, and certain events open Special Enrollment Periods. Because plans refile pricing every year, comparing each fall is the only way to be sure you still have the best deal. Do not wait until the deadline — call 1-800-MEDIGAP early so a licensed agent can compare your options without the rush.

faqs.q: What is the best way to compare Part D plans?
faqs.a: Compare by total annual cost for your specific drug list — premium, deductible, and copays combined — not by premium alone. Confirm each medication is on the formulary and your pharmacy is in-network. A free comparison through 1-800-MEDIGAP does this for you.

faqs.q: When can I compare and switch plans for 2026?
faqs.a: The Annual Enrollment Period runs October 15 to December 7, 2025, for coverage starting January 1, 2026. This is the main window to compare and change plans. Some life events open a Special Enrollment Period to switch outside that window.

faqs.q: Why does my best plan change each year?
faqs.a: Plans refile premiums, formularies, tier placements, and pharmacy networks every year. A drug that was cheap on your plan in 2025 may move to a higher tier in 2026, so comparing annually is the only way to confirm you still have the lowest-cost option.

faqs.q: Is help comparing Part D plans free?
faqs.a: Yes. Licensed agents at 1-800-MEDIGAP compare available 2026 Part D plans against your medication list at no cost to you. They check formularies, total costs, and pharmacy networks, then help you enroll in the plan that fits best.

faqs.q: What information do I need to compare plans?
faqs.a: Have your full medication list with doses and frequencies, your preferred pharmacy, and your ZIP code ready. With those, an agent can compare formularies, total annual costs, and networks across available plans. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP and we will handle the comparison.
